Analyzing the Impact of COVID-19 on New Orleans Education¶

Shania Phillips and Maria Aponte Rosero¶

https://sphil5.github.io/¶

In [2]:
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).

Project Goals¶

The COVID-19 pandemic caused widespread disruptions to daily life, including significant changes to how students experienced education. This project aims to examine the impact of COVID-19 on public school education in New Orleans, Louisiana. Specifically, it analyzes changes in school performance scores (SPS) and student progress indices across multiple schools, comparing trends from the pre-pandemic period (2016–2019) to the post-pandemic years (2020–2025). In addition to this, the project also explores variation across schools to determine whether recovery has been uniform or uneven. By evaluating both overall performance and measures of student growth, this analysis seeks to identify the extent of academic disruption and assess patterns of recovery across schools over time.

Research Questions:

  1. Is student progress index a significant predictor of school performance score (SPS)?
  2. Did COVID-19 cause a statistically significant decline in average school performance scores?

Collaboration Plan¶

For this project, we plan to meet once a week to discuss progress, refine our research questions, and plan the next steps of our analysis. Between meetings, we will communicate via text to address questions and provide support as needed. We will use a shared GitHub repository to manage our code and data, allowing us to track changes, organize files, and ensure that both collaborators are working with the most up-to-date version of the project. Responsibilities will be divided across tasks such as data cleaning, visualization, and interpretation, with us both contributing to each stage of the analysis.

Dataset 1: Pre-COVID Data¶

The first dataset used in this analysis consists of school performance score (SPS) data obtained from the Louisiana Department of Education. We collected SPS datasets for the years 2016–2019, representing the pre-COVID period. Each dataset contains school-level performance metrics, including overall performance scores and related indicators used to evaluate academic achievement. To prepare the data for analysis, the individual yearly datasets were cleaned and standardized to ensure consistent column names and formats across years. These datasets were then combined into a single dataframe covering the full 2016–2019 period. This consolidated dataset allows for efficient comparison of school performance trends prior to the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Data Cleaning (ETL)¶

To prepare the datasets for analysis, we performed several cleaning and transformation steps to ensure consistency and usability across years:

  • Standardized all column names to lowercase with underscores
  • Melted wide-format yearly data into a tidy long format (one row per school per year)
  • Extracted the numeric year from column names and constructed a readable school_year label
  • Removed duplicate rows created by overlapping year columns across datasets
  • Retained only the three columns needed for analysis: school_name, school_year, and school_performance_score_sps
In [3]:
# Helper function to standardize column names across all yearly datasets
def clean_columns(df):
    df.columns = (
        df.columns
        .str.lower()
        .str.strip()
        .str.replace("\n", "_")
        .str.replace(" ", "_")
        .str.replace(r"[()]", "", regex=True)
    )
    return df
In [4]:
import os

os.listdir('/content')

In [5]:
# @title Cleaning the 2016 dataset:

# Load 2016 dataset and skip the empty header rows
df_2016 = pd.read_excel("/content/sphil5.github.io/data/2016-school-performance-scores.xlsx", header=2)
# Standardize column names
df_2016 = clean_columns(df_2016)
# Rename column for consistency
df_2016 = df_2016.rename(columns={"school": "school_name"})
# Convert wide format to long format
df_2016_long = pd.melt(
    df_2016,
    id_vars=["school_name"],
    value_vars=["2016_annual_sps", "2015_annual_sps"],
    var_name="year",
    value_name="school_performance_score_sps"
)
# Extract year and create school_year format
df_2016_long["year"] = df_2016_long["year"].str.extract(r"(\d{4})").astype(int)
df_2016_long["school_year"] = df_2016_long["year"].astype(str) + "-" + (df_2016_long["year"] + 1).astype(str)
# Drop temporary column
df_2016_long = df_2016_long.drop(columns=["year"])
In [6]:
# @title Cleaning the 2017 dataset:

# Load 2017 dataset and skip the empty header rows
df_2017 = pd.read_excel("/content/sphil5.github.io//data/2017-school-performance-score-summary.xlsx", header=2)
# Standardize column names
df_2017 = clean_columns(df_2017)
# Rename column for consistency
df_2017 = df_2017.rename(columns={"school": "school_name"})
# Convert wide format to long format
df_2017_long = pd.melt(
    df_2017,
    id_vars=["school_name"],
    value_vars=["2017_final_sps", "2016_annual_sps"],
    var_name="year",
    value_name="school_performance_score_sps"
)
df_2017_long["year"] = df_2017_long["year"].astype(str)
# Extract year and create school_year format
df_2017_long["year"] = df_2017_long["year"].str.extract(r"(\d{4})").astype(int)
df_2017_long["school_year"] = df_2017_long["year"].astype(str) + "-" + (df_2017_long["year"] + 1).astype(str)
# Drop temporary column
df_2017_long = df_2017_long.drop(columns=["year"])
In [7]:
# @title Cleaning the 2018 dataset:

# Load 2018 dataset and skip the empty header rows
df_2018 = pd.read_excel("/content/sphil5.github.io/data/2018-school-performance-scores.xlsx", header=3)
# Standardize column names
df_2018 = clean_columns(df_2018)
# Rename column for consistency
df_2018 = df_2018.rename(columns={"school": "school_name"})
# Convert wide format to long format
df_2018_long = pd.melt(
    df_2018,
    id_vars=["school_name"],
    value_vars=["2018_sps__old_formula", "2017_sps__old_formula"],
    var_name="year",
    value_name="school_performance_score_sps"
)
df_2018_long["year"] = df_2018_long["year"].astype(str)
# Extract year and create school_year format
df_2018_long["year"] = df_2018_long["year"].str.extract(r"(\d{4})").astype(int)
df_2018_long["school_year"] = df_2018_long["year"].astype(str) + "-" + (df_2018_long["year"] + 1).astype(str)
# Drop temporary column
df_2018_long = df_2018_long.drop(columns=["year"])
In [8]:
# @title Cleaning the 2019 dataset:

# Load 2019 dataset and skip the empty header rows
df_2019 = pd.read_excel("/content/sphil5.github.io/data/2019-school-performance-scores.xlsx", header=3)
# Standardize column names
df_2019 = clean_columns(df_2019)
# Rename column for consistency
df_2019 = df_2019.rename(columns={"school": "school_name"})
# Convert wide format to long format
df_2019_long = pd.melt(
    df_2019,
    id_vars=["school_name"],
    value_vars=["2019_sps", "2018_sps"],
    var_name="year",
    value_name="school_performance_score_sps"
)
df_2019_long["year"] = df_2019_long["year"].astype(str)
# Extract year and create school_year format
df_2019_long["year"] = df_2019_long["year"].str.extract(r"(\d{4})").astype(int)
df_2019_long["school_year"] = df_2019_long["year"].astype(str) + "-" + (df_2019_long["year"] + 1).astype(str)
# Drop temporary column
df_2019_long = df_2019_long.drop(columns=["year"])
In [9]:
# @title Combining datasets into a DataFrame:

# Combine all pre-COVID yearly DataFrames into one tidy dataset and remove duplicates
df_pre_covid = pd.concat([
    df_2016_long,
    df_2017_long,
    df_2018_long,
    df_2019_long
], ignore_index=True)
# Remove duplicate rows
df_pre_covid = df_pre_covid.drop_duplicates()
# Keep only the three columns needed for analysis
df_pre_covid = df_pre_covid[[
    "school_name",
    "school_year",
    "school_performance_score_sps"
]]

# Convert SPS to numeric, coercing any non-numeric values to NaN
df_pre_covid["school_performance_score_sps"] = pd.to_numeric(
    df_pre_covid["school_performance_score_sps"], errors="coerce"
)

# Drop rows with missing SPS values
df_pre_covid = df_pre_covid.dropna(subset=["school_performance_score_sps"])

print("Pre-COVID dataset shape:", df_pre_covid.shape)
print("\nMissing values:")
print(df_pre_covid.isna().sum())
print("\nData types:")
print(df_pre_covid.dtypes)
print("\nRecords per school year:")
print(df_pre_covid["school_year"].value_counts().sort_index())

Pre-COVID Performance Trends¶

Before analyzing the impact of COVID-19, we examine school performance trends during the pre-pandemic years (2015–2019). By visualizing the trend over time, we can establish whether performance was stable, improving, or already declining before the pandemic hit. This provides a baseline for understanding how performance changed after the onset of the pandemic.

In [11]:
# Calculate average SPS for each pre-COVID school year
avg_pre = (
    df_pre_covid
    .groupby("school_year")["school_performance_score_sps"]
    .mean()
    .sort_index()
)

avg_pre.plot(marker="o", figsize=(8, 4))
plt.title("Pre-COVID School Performance Trends (2015–2019)")
plt.xlabel("School Year")
plt.ylabel("Average SPS")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Display values for verification
avg_pre
Out[11]:
school_performance_score_sps
school_year
2015-2016 79.944308
2016-2017 81.816630
2017-2018 80.038444
2018-2019 77.484400
2019-2020 73.659826

While there is a slight increase between 2015–2016 and 2016–2017, school performance scores begin to decline in the following years, with a more noticeable drop leading into 2019–2020. This suggests that school performance may have already been decreasing prior to the pandemic and were not initially stable. However, the sharper decline in 2019–2020, the year in which the onset of the pandemic occurred, indicates that COVID-19 likely intensified this pattern, contributing to a more significant disruption in academic performance.

Dataset 2: Post-COVID Data¶

The second dataset was obtained from NOLA Public Schools. This dataset includes key academic metrics for New Orleans public schools from 2020 onward to the most recent completed school year (2024–2025). For this analysis, we focus on school performance score (SPS) and progress index. These variables allow us to evaluate both academic outcomes and student growth in the years following COVID-19.

Data Cleaning (ETL)¶

We cleaned up this dataset by performing the following steps:

  • Standardized column names to lowercase with underscores
  • Renamed the SPS column to match the pre-COVID dataset for easy concatenation
  • Removed rows with missing school performance scores
  • Dropped columns that were almost entirely missing (nola-ps_spri, nola-ps_spri_letter_grade)
  • Converted SPS and progress index to numeric types
  • Verified data types and remaining missing values
In [12]:
# Load dataset
df_postcovid = pd.read_excel("/content/sphil5.github.io/data/academic_metrics.xlsx")
df_postcovid.head()

Data Cleaning (ETL)¶

We cleaned up this dataset by performing a few different tasks.

First, we changed the column names to make them easier to reference in code.

Next, we removed any missing data from the school performance scores column to focus on the information present for our statistics and visualization.

Lastly, we got rid of columns that had most of its data missing since it would not be as useful to our investigation.

In [13]:
# Standardize column names to lowercase with underscores
df_postcovid.columns = df_postcovid.columns.str.lower().str.replace(" ", "_")

# Rename the SPS column to match the pre-COVID dataframe
df_postcovid = df_postcovid.rename(columns={
    "school_performance_score_(sps)": "school_performance_score_sps"
})

# Drop rows with missing performance scores
df_postcovid = df_postcovid.dropna(subset=["school_performance_score_sps"])

# Drop columns with the majority of data missing
df_postcovid = df_postcovid.drop(columns=[
    "nola-ps_spri",
    "nola-ps_spri_letter_grade"
], errors="ignore")  # errors='ignore' prevents crash if columns are already absent

# Convert key numeric columns
df_postcovid["school_performance_score_sps"] = pd.to_numeric(
    df_postcovid["school_performance_score_sps"], errors="coerce"
)
if "progress_index" in df_postcovid.columns:
    df_postcovid["progress_index"] = pd.to_numeric(
        df_postcovid["progress_index"], errors="coerce"
    )

print("Post-COVID dataset shape:", df_postcovid.shape)
print("\nMissing values:")
print(df_postcovid.isna().sum())
print("\nData types:")
print(df_postcovid.dtypes)

Interesting Statistics¶

Key statistics from the post-COVID dataset include:

  • There are 75 unique schools included in this dataset.
  • The average performance score has increased since the 2020–2021 school year, rising from approximately 64.53 to 74.67.
  • Benjamin Franklin High School had the highest performance score of 133.9, occurring in the 2024–2025 school year.
  • Thurgood Marshall had the lowest performance score of 40.9, occurring in the 2020–2021 school year.

These statistics inform us of when the highest and lowest performance scores occurred, as well as the overall average across years. This gives us an initial indication of performance score recovery trends since the pandemic.

In [15]:
# Number of unique schools (each school appears multiple times across years)
num_schools = df_postcovid["school_name"].nunique()

# Average SPS per school year
avg_performance_per_year = df_postcovid.groupby("school_year")["school_performance_score_sps"].mean()

# School with the highest and lowest SPS
max_score = df_postcovid.loc[df_postcovid["school_performance_score_sps"].idxmax()]
min_score = df_postcovid.loc[df_postcovid["school_performance_score_sps"].idxmin()]

print("Number of schools:", num_schools)
print("\nAverage performance score per year:")
print(avg_performance_per_year.to_string())
print("\nHighest SPS — School:", max_score["school_name"], "| Year:", max_score["school_year"], "| Score:", max_score["school_performance_score_sps"])
print("Lowest SPS  — School:", min_score["school_name"], "| Year:", min_score["school_year"], "| Score:", min_score["school_performance_score_sps"])

school_year

Highest SPS — School: Benjamin Franklin High School | Year: 2024-2025 | Score: 133.9
Lowest SPS  — School: Success @ Thurgood Marshall | Year: 2020-2021 | Score: 40.9

Post-COVID Performance Trends¶

This line graph shows the average School Performance Score for each year from 2020–2021 onward. It focuses the view on the recovery period, allowing us to assess whether and how quickly scores rebounded after the initial COVID-19 disruption.

In [16]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Plot the change in the average performance score throughout the  school years.
avg_performance_per_year.plot(marker="o", figsize=(8, 4))

# Label the graph
plt.title("Average School Performance Score by Year")
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Average Performance Score")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

In this section we combine both datasets and explore trends, distributions, and relationships in the data to inform our modeling decisions.

In [17]:
# Combine pre- and post-COVID datasets into one unified DataFrame
# df_pre_covid has: school_name, school_year, school_performance_score_sps
# df_postcovid has all of the above plus additional columns (progress_index, school_type, etc.)
df_all = pd.concat([df_pre_covid, df_postcovid], ignore_index=True)

# Verify school_year is consistent (string format) across both datasets
df_all["school_year"] = df_all["school_year"].astype(str)

print("Combined dataset shape:", df_all.shape)
print("\nSchool years present:")
print(sorted(df_all["school_year"].unique()))

Combined dataset shape: (8141, 18)

School years present:
['2015-2016', '2016-2017', '2017-2018', '2018-2019', '2019-2020', '2020-2021', '2021-2022', '2022-2023', '2023-2024', '2024-2025']

Missing Data Analysis¶

Before conducting exploratory analysis and modeling, we examine missing data across the combined dataset. This helps ensure that our results are not biased and informs how we handle incomplete observations in later steps.

In [18]:
missing_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Missing Values": df_all.isnull().sum(),
    "Percent Missing": (df_all.isnull().sum() / len(df_all)) * 100
}).sort_values(by="Percent Missing", ascending=False)

missing_table
Out[18]:
Missing Values Percent Missing
graduation_rate 8011 98.403145

school_name 0 0.000000
school_year 0 0.000000
school_performance_score_sps 0 0.000000

Most missing values occur in variables that are not present in both datasets, such as progress_index, which is only available in the post-COVID data. Since our models rely only on variables available at the time of analysis, we handle missing data by filtering to complete cases when necessary rather than imputing values.

School Performance Before and After COVID¶

This line graph plots the average SPS across all schools for every school year from 2015–2016 through 2024–2025. A dashed vertical line marks the start of COVID-19 (2019–2020). This is the central visualization of our analysis — it allows us to directly compare the trajectory of school performance before, during, and after the pandemic disruption.

In [19]:
# Calculate average SPS per school year across the combined dataset
avg_all = df_all.groupby("school_year")["school_performance_score_sps"].mean().sort_index()

# Get the integer position of the COVID start year for the vertical line
years_list = list(avg_all.index)
covid_index = years_list.index("2019-2020") if "2019-2020" in years_list else None

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
avg_all.plot(marker="o", ax=ax)
if covid_index is not None:
    ax.axvline(x=covid_index, color="red", linestyle="--", label="COVID-19 Start (2019–2020)")
ax.legend()
ax.set_title("Average School Performance Score Before and After COVID-19")
ax.set_xlabel("School Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Average SPS")
plt.xticks(range(len(years_list)), years_list, rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Average School Performance Scores Before and After COVID-19¶

This bar chart compares the average School Performance Scores (SPS) between the pre-COVID (2015–2019) and post-COVID (2020–2025) periods. While post-COVID scores appear to be approaching pre-pandemic levels, they do not fully exceed them, suggesting a partial but incomplete recovery. This pattern indicates that although schools have made progress in regaining performance, the long-term impact of COVID-19 may still be present.

In [20]:
df_all["period"] = df_all["school_year"].apply(
    lambda x: "Pre-COVID" if x < "2019-2020" else "Post-COVID"
)

avg_period = df_all.groupby("period")["school_performance_score_sps"].mean()

# Fix order
avg_period = avg_period.reindex(["Pre-COVID", "Post-COVID"])

avg_period.plot(kind="bar", edgecolor="black")
plt.title("Average School Performance: Pre vs Post COVID")
plt.ylabel("Average SPS")
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.show()

SPS Distribution by School Year (Box Plot)¶

This box plot shows the spread of individual school performance scores for each post-COVID school year. Unlike the line graph of averages, the box plot reveals the median, interquartile range, and outliers for each year. This lets us see not only whether the average improved, but whether the improvement was broad-based or concentrated in a few high-performing schools.

In [21]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.boxplot(
    data=df_postcovid,
    x="school_year",
    y="school_performance_score_sps"
)
plt.title("SPS Distribution by School Year (Post-COVID)")
plt.xlabel("School Year")
plt.ylabel("School Performance Score (SPS)")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Summary Statistics Table: SPS by School Year¶

This cross-tabulation of summary statistics (mean, median, standard deviation, count) by school year gives a precise numerical picture of how SPS has changed over time. The median is especially useful here alongside the mean, as it is less sensitive to outlier schools like Benjamin Franklin High School.

In [22]:
# Group by school year and compute measures of central tendency and spread
sps_summary = df_postcovid.groupby("school_year")["school_performance_score_sps"].agg(
    count="count",
    mean="mean",
    median="median",
    std="std",
    min="min",
    max="max"
).round(2)

print("Summary Statistics: SPS by School Year")
sps_summary

Summary Statistics: SPS by School Year
Out[22]:
count mean median std min max
school_year
2020-2021 65 64.53 57.8 19.41 40.9 130.3
2021-2022 70 66.48 61.2 18.38 43.3 132.4
2022-2023 71 70.57 67.1 16.76 45.4 127.5
2023-2024 73 74.15 70.0 16.27 52.1 130.6
2024-2025 73 74.67 70.1 17.71 45.0 133.9

Relationship Between Student Progress and School Performance¶

This scatterplot examines the relationship between a school's progress index (a measure of how much students grew academically during the year) and its overall SPS. A positive relationship here would suggest that student growth is a meaningful driver of school performance — and would motivate the use of progress index as a predictor in our regression model. Each point is a school in a given year, colored by school year.

In [23]:
# Filter to rows that have both progress_index and SPS available
df_analysis = df_postcovid.dropna(subset=["progress_index", "school_performance_score_sps"])

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))
sns.scatterplot(
    data=df_analysis,
    x="progress_index",
    y="school_performance_score_sps",
    hue="school_year",
    alpha=0.6
)
plt.title("Relationship Between Student Progress Index and School Performance")
plt.xlabel("Progress Index")
plt.ylabel("School Performance Score (SPS)")
plt.legend(title="School Year", bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Post-COVID Progress vs. Performance by School Type¶

This scatterplot breaks down the progress-performance relationship by school type (e.g., charter vs. traditional). Comparing school types allows us to assess whether the recovery from COVID-19 was uneven across different institutional structures. If school types cluster differently, this variable may be worth including as a control in our models.

In [24]:
if "school_type" in df_analysis.columns:
    plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))
    sns.scatterplot(
        data=df_analysis,
        x="progress_index",
        y="school_performance_score_sps",
        hue="school_type",
        alpha=0.6
    )
    plt.title("Post-COVID: Progress Index vs. Performance by School Type")
    plt.xlabel("Progress Index")
    plt.ylabel("School Performance Score (SPS)")
    plt.legend(title="School Type", bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc="upper left")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
else:
    print("'school_type' column not found in dataset — skipping this plot.")

The scatterplots above show a positive relationship between student progress and overall school performance. Schools with higher progress index scores tend to have higher SPS values. In the post-COVID years, this relationship remains visible, suggesting that recovery in school performance is closely tied to student learning growth. This indicates that differences in recovery across schools may be driven by how effectively they supported student progress during and after the pandemic.

Model Questions¶

The exploratory data analysis above supports the following two modeling approaches:

Model Question 1: Predicting School Performance from Student Progress¶

Question: Is student progress index a significant predictor of school performance score (SPS) in post-COVID New Orleans schools?

  • Dependent Variable: school_performance_score_sps
  • Independent Variable: progress_index
  • Methodology: Simple linear regression. We will fit a least-squares regression line, report the coefficient and R² value, and evaluate statistical significance via the p-value of the slope.
  • Evaluation: R² (proportion of variance explained), RMSE (average prediction error), and a residual plot to check for violations of linearity.
  • EDA Support: Graph 6 shows a clear positive relationship between progress_index and SPS, suggesting that a linear model is a reasonable starting point.

Model Question 2: Impact of COVID-19 on School Performance¶

Question: Did COVID-19 cause a statistically significant decline in average school performance scores in New Orleans public schools?

  • Dependent Variable: school_performance_score_sps
  • Independent Variable: period — a binary variable coded as 0 for Pre-COVID (2015–2019) and 1 for Post-COVID (2020–2025)
  • Methodology: Two-sample independent t-test (H₀: mean SPS pre-COVID = mean SPS post-COVID; H₁: mean SPS pre-COVID > mean SPS post-COVID). We will report the t-statistic, p-value, and 95% confidence interval for the difference in means.
  • Evaluation: Statistical significance at α = 0.05, and a visual comparison using overlapping histograms or a box plot of pre- vs. post-COVID SPS.
  • EDA Support: Graph 2 shows a visible drop in average SPS at the COVID start year, which motivates testing whether this difference is statistically significant rather than due to chance variation.

Model Justification¶

Both models are directly informed by the EDA conducted above. The consistent upward trend in average SPS post-COVID (Graph 3), the visible drop at the COVID onset (Graph 2), and the positive progress-performance relationship (Graph 6) together provide strong justification for pursuing these two quantitative questions in subsequent milestones.

Linear Regression Model: Predicting SPS from Progress Index¶

In [25]:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_squared_error
from scipy.stats import linregress
import numpy as np

# Remove missing values
df_model = df_postcovid.dropna(
    subset=["progress_index", "school_performance_score_sps"]
)

X = df_model[["progress_index"]]
y = df_model["school_performance_score_sps"]

# Fit model
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X, y)

# Predictions
y_pred = model.predict(X)

# Metrics
r2 = r2_score(y, y_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y, y_pred))

print("Slope:", model.coef_[0])
print("Intercept:", model.intercept_)
print("R²:", r2)
print("RMSE:", rmse)

Slope: 0.7181966868870339
Intercept: 7.444197999711044
R²: 0.30339430238665344
RMSE: 15.082545772683781
In [26]:
# Perform linear regression to measure the relationship between student progress index and school performance score (SPS)
slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = linregress(
    df_model["progress_index"],
    df_model["school_performance_score_sps"]
)

# Display the p-value
print("P-value:", p_value)

P-value: 3.68784430989197e-29
In [27]:
# @title Visualization
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

sns.regplot(
    data=df_model,
    x="progress_index",
    y="school_performance_score_sps",
    line_kws={"color": "red"}
)

plt.title("Linear Regression: Progress Index vs SPS")
plt.xlabel("Progress Index")
plt.ylabel("School Performance Score")
plt.show()

The regression model shows a positive relationship between progress index and SPS. The statistically significant p-value suggests that student growth is a meaningful predictor of school performance. The R² value indicates how much variation in SPS can be explained by progress index alone. While the model does not capture all factors affecting school performance, it demonstrates that academic growth was strongly associated with school recovery after COVID-19.

In [28]:
# Calculate the prediction errors made by the model
residuals = y - y_pred

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(x=y_pred, y=residuals)

plt.axhline(0, color='red', linestyle='--')

plt.title("Residual Plot")
plt.xlabel("Predicted SPS")
plt.ylabel("Residuals")
plt.show()

The residual plot shows that errors are randomly distributed around zero, suggesting that a linear model is appropriate. While some spread exists, there is no clear pattern indicating major violations of model assumptions.

Statistical Test: Comparing Pre- and Post-COVID SPS¶

In [29]:
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind

# Separate SPS into pre-COVID and post-COVID groups
pre_scores = df_all[
    df_all["period"] == "Pre-COVID"
]["school_performance_score_sps"]

post_scores = df_all[
    df_all["period"] == "Post-COVID"
]["school_performance_score_sps"]

# Perform an independent two-sample t-test
t_stat, p_value = ttest_ind(
    pre_scores,
    post_scores,
    equal_var=False
)

# Display
print("T-statistic:", t_stat)
print("P-value:", p_value)

T-statistic: 12.514975337630377
P-value: 4.442416434694247e-35
In [30]:
# @title Visualization
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

sns.boxplot(
    data=df_all,
    x="period",
    y="school_performance_score_sps"
)

plt.title("Pre vs Post COVID SPS")
plt.xlabel("Period")
plt.ylabel("School Performance Score")
plt.show()

The t-test results indicate whether the difference between pre- and post-COVID school performance scores is statistically significant. A small p-value would suggest that the observed decline in SPS is unlikely to be due to random variation alone. This supports the conclusion that COVID-19 had a measurable impact on school performance outcomes in New Orleans public schools.

Segmented Regression Around Covid-19¶

In [31]:
from scipy.stats import linregress

# Create numeric year index
avg_all_df = avg_all.reset_index()
avg_all_df.columns = ["school_year", "avg_sps"]
avg_all_df["time"] = range(len(avg_all_df))

# Split pre/post COVID
pre = avg_all_df[avg_all_df["school_year"] < "2019-2020"]
post = avg_all_df[avg_all_df["school_year"] >= "2019-2020"]

# Separate regressions
pre_reg = linregress(pre["time"], pre["avg_sps"])
post_reg = linregress(post["time"], post["avg_sps"])

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))

plt.scatter(avg_all_df["time"], avg_all_df["avg_sps"])

# Pre trend line
plt.plot(
    pre["time"],
    pre_reg.intercept + pre_reg.slope * pre["time"],
)

# Post trend line
plt.plot(
    post["time"],
    post_reg.intercept + post_reg.slope * post["time"],
)

plt.axvline(
    x=pre["time"].max(),
    color="red",
    linestyle="--",
    label="COVID Onset"
)

plt.xticks(
    avg_all_df["time"],
    avg_all_df["school_year"],
    rotation=45
)

plt.title("Segmented Regression Before and After COVID-19")
plt.xlabel("School Year")
plt.ylabel("Average SPS")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The segmented regression shows that SPS trends differed before and after the onset of COVID-19. The slope of the post-COVID regression line suggests a recovery trend, while the discontinuity near 2019–2020 indicates a structural disruption associated with the pandemic. Although this analysis does not prove causality, it provides evidence that COVID-19 corresponded with a meaningful shift in academic performance trends.

Conclusions¶

This analysis examined the impact of COVID-19 on school performance in New Orleans public schools using pre-pandemic (2015–2019) and post-pandemic (2020–2025) data. Through exploratory analysis and statistical modeling, we evaluated both overall performance trends and the role of student growth in recovery.

The linear regression results show a clear positive relationship between student progress index and school performance score (SPS). The statistically significant p-value suggests that student growth is an important predictor of school performance, with higher progress associated with higher SPS in the post-COVID period.

The t-test results indicate that the decline in performance at the onset of COVID-19 is statistically significant, supporting the conclusion that the pandemic had a measurable negative impact on school performance rather than the change being due to random variation.

The segmented regression further shows a structural break in trends around 2019–2020. While performance declines sharply at the start of COVID-19, the post-COVID trend shows signs of recovery, though not at the same trajectory as before the pandemic.

Overall, these results suggest that New Orleans schools have made progress in recovering from pandemic-related disruptions, but the effects of COVID-19 are still present and recovery remains ongoing and uneven.

Limitations¶

This analysis has several limitations. First, the data is observational, so we cannot make causal claims about the impact of COVID-19. Second, the dataset is limited to New Orleans public schools, which may limit generalizability to other regions.

Additionally, not all variables are consistently available across time periods. For example, the progress index only exists in the post-COVID data, and changes in SPS calculations over time may affect direct comparisons across years.

Finally, the models used are relatively simple and do not account for other influencing factors such as school funding, demographics, or policy changes. Future work could incorporate additional variables or more advanced models to better understand differences in recovery across schools.
